sentence: Subjective:

HPI: The patient, Julia, is a 68-year-old female who has been experiencing chest pain and shortness of breath, similar to symptoms she had during her previous heart attack, which led to the placement of an RCA stent in 2018.
She has a history of coronary artery disease, hypertension, and diabetes.
She reports being able to walk her dog daily, but has noticed some swelling in her legs since being discharged from the hospital.
She has been taking her prescribed medications, including aspirin, brilinta, lipitor, and toprol, but has not been adhering to a low-sodium diet.
She consumes high-sodium foods such as pizza with pepperoni, which she enjoys.
Past Medical History: The patient has a history of coronary artery disease, hypertension, and diabetes.
She has undergone an RCA stent placement in 2018 and a recent inferior STEMI, for which she required another stent.
Review of Systems: The patient reports no chest pain or shortness of breath, but notes some swelling in her legs since discharge from the hospital.
Current Medications: Aspirin 81mg daily, brilinta 90mg twice daily, lipitor 80mg daily, and toprol 50mg daily.
Objective:
Vital Signs: Not mentioned.
Physical Exam: The patient's neck exam was unremarkable with no jugular venous distention or carotid bruits.
A 3/6 systolic ejection murmur was heard at the left base.
The lungs were clear, but the extremities showed some lower limb edema, which was pitting 1+ on the right radial artery.
The cath site was clean, dry, and intact without hematoma, and the right radial artery pulses were palpable.
Test Results: The patient's EKG showed a normal sinus rhythm and good R wave progression, with evolutionary changes anticipated after a STEMI.
The echo showed a reduced ejection fraction of 35%, moderate mitral regurgitation, and newly reduced left ventricular dysfunction.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has coronary artery disease, reduced left ventricular dysfunction, and moderate mitral regurgitation.
Problem List: Coronary artery disease; reduced left ventricular dysfunction; moderate mitral regurgitation; hypertension; diabetes; acute heart failure.
Plan:
I prescribed lasix 40mg daily for acute heart failure and aldactone 12.5mg daily.
I will refer the patient to cardiac rehab for education and exercise counseling.
I will also refer the patient to nutrition counseling to discuss diet options and potential alternatives to high-sodium foods.
I will repeat an echo in 2 months to reassess the patient's cardiac function.
Follow-up: The patient will be seen in 2 months to reassess her cardiac function and adjust her treatment plan as necessary.
